SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates the proposed Word2Vec-based method on two datasets (IMDB and Stanford Sentiment Treebank) and in both unsupervised and semi-supervised conditions, and compares against the PMI-IR baseline, which supports the core claims to a degree. However, key experimental details are missing or limited in the summary: evaluation metrics are not specified, hyperparameters are not reported, code is not mentioned, and only one baseline is used. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are not described and discussion of limitations focuses mainly on PMI-IR rather than the new method, so reproducibility and analysis depth are insufficient for a higher score. | B(3): The paper runs experiments on at least two datasets (IMDB and the Stanford Sentiment Treebank) and compares its unsupervised and semi‑supervised Word2Vec/PCA approach against the PMI‑IR baseline, which supports the core claim of improved performance. However, key experimental details are missing from the summary: evaluation metrics are not specified, hyperparameters and code are not reported, and no ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of dimensionality, seed selection, corpus size) are described. Related work coverage is said to be thorough and the PMI‑IR limitations are discussed, but the authors do not appear to analyze their own method’s failure cases or provide sufficient reproducibility detail, so the evaluation is adequate but incomplete.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates the proposed Word2Vec-based method on two datasets (IMDB and Stanford Sentiment Treebank) and in both unsupervised and semi-supervised conditions, and compares against the PMI-IR baseline, which supports the core claims to a degree. However, key experimental details are missing or limited in the summary: evaluation metrics are not specified, hyperparameters are not reported, code is not mentioned, and only one baseline is used. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are not described and discussion of limitations focuses mainly on PMI-IR rather than the new method, so reproducibility and analysis depth are insufficient for a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs experiments on at least two datasets (IMDB and the Stanford Sentiment Treebank) and compares its unsupervised and semi‑supervised Word2Vec/PCA approach against the PMI‑IR baseline, which supports the core claim of improved performance. However, key experimental details are missing from the summary: evaluation metrics are not specified, hyperparameters and code are not reported, and no ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of dimensionality, seed selection, corpus size) are described. Related work coverage is said to be thorough and the PMI‑IR limitations are discussed, but the authors do not appear to analyze their own method’s failure cases or provide sufficient reproducibility detail, so the evaluation is adequate but incomplete.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—unsupervised sentiment orientation using distributional semantic (word-embedding) models—and evaluates on standard sentiment datasets (IMDB, Stanford Sentiment Treebank), so the topic and audience fit a top NLP/ML venue. The problem formulation (computing sentiment axes in vector space and comparing to PMI-IR) and the use of unsupervised and semi-supervised conditions are appropriate, but missing reported evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, and code raise reproducibility concerns that are important for top-tier standards. Overall the work is well positioned (explicit comparison to Turney 2002, thorough related work) but has minor scope/reproducibility issues that keep it from being a perfect fit. | B(4): The paper's topic — measuring word-level sentiment orientation using distributional semantic/vector-space methods and word embeddings — squarely fits the NLP/ML community and would interest a top-tier venue (datasets used include IMDB and the Stanford Sentiment Treebank and the baseline is the classic PMI-IR method). It is targeted at the right audience and framed as an unsupervised/semi-supervised contribution to sentiment analysis. However, reporting gaps (no evaluation metrics specified, hyperparameters not reported, and code availability not mentioned) mean it does not fully meet the methodological and reproducibility standards typically expected at top conferences, which is a minor scope concern.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—unsupervised sentiment orientation using distributional semantic (word-embedding) models—and evaluates on standard sentiment datasets (IMDB, Stanford Sentiment Treebank), so the topic and audience fit a top NLP/ML venue. The problem formulation (computing sentiment axes in vector space and comparing to PMI-IR) and the use of unsupervised and semi-supervised conditions are appropriate, but missing reported evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, and code raise reproducibility concerns that are important for top-tier standards. Overall the work is well positioned (explicit comparison to Turney 2002, thorough related work) but has minor scope/reproducibility issues that keep it from being a perfect fit.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's topic — measuring word-level sentiment orientation using distributional semantic/vector-space methods and word embeddings — squarely fits the NLP/ML community and would interest a top-tier venue (datasets used include IMDB and the Stanford Sentiment Treebank and the baseline is the classic PMI-IR method). It is targeted at the right audience and framed as an unsupervised/semi-supervised contribution to sentiment analysis. However, reporting gaps (no evaluation metrics specified, hyperparameters not reported, and code availability not mentioned) mean it does not fully meet the methodological and reproducibility standards typically expected at top conferences, which is a minor scope concern.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through method (vector-space construction, PCA sentiment axis, SO formula) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and key formulas (e.g., SO(w)=CosDst(Vec_pos,w)-CosDst(Vec_neg,w)) are clearly presented, and writing quality and related-work coverage are good. Minor presentation issues remain: evaluation metrics are not specified, hyperparameters are not reported, and code availability is not mentioned, which reduces reproducibility and leaves some experimental details ambiguous. | B(4): The paper appears well organized with a clear methodological flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (novel vector-space sentiment orientation and comparison to PMI-IR). Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough, which supports readability. However, some presentation details that affect clarity and reproducibility are missing or not reported in the summary (hyperparameters not reported, evaluation metrics not specified, and effectiveness of figures/tables not mentioned), preventing an exceptional score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through method (vector-space construction, PCA sentiment axis, SO formula) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and key formulas (e.g., SO(w)=CosDst(Vec_pos,w)-CosDst(Vec_neg,w)) are clearly presented, and writing quality and related-work coverage are good. Minor presentation issues remain: evaluation metrics are not specified, hyperparameters are not reported, and code availability is not mentioned, which reduces reproducibility and leaves some experimental details ambiguous.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well organized with a clear methodological flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early (novel vector-space sentiment orientation and comparison to PMI-IR). Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough, which supports readability. However, some presentation details that affect clarity and reproducibility are missing or not reported in the summary (hyperparameters not reported, evaluation metrics not specified, and effectiveness of figures/tables not mentioned), preventing an exceptional score.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable innovation by replacing PMI-IR with a distributional semantic (Word2Vec-based) method to measure sentiment orientation and by deriving a sentiment axis via PCA on point-word vectors. Unlike Turney (2002), it computes sentiment orientations for all vocabulary words rather than only phrase patterns and is claimed to outperform PMI-IR while being robust to data sparsity and smaller datasets. These points clearly differentiate the contribution from prior work and represent a non-trivial methodological shift, though the approach builds on existing vector-space ideas rather than introducing an entirely new theoretical framework. | B(4): The paper proposes a notable novel use of distributional semantic models—building a high-dimensional word embedding space, using PCA to derive a sentiment axis, and defining sentiment orientation as the difference in cosine distance to averaged positive/negative reference vectors—which is different from Turney’s PMI-IR phrase-based approach. It clearly differentiates its contribution (unsupervised Word2Vec-based method applicable to all vocabulary words and robust to smaller datasets) from prior work, and while it builds on existing embedding techniques, the specific pipeline (PCA-derived axis + reference vectors + SO formulation) is a non-trivial and informative extension that would be surprising to experts familiar only with PMI-IR.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable innovation by replacing PMI-IR with a distributional semantic (Word2Vec-based) method to measure sentiment orientation and by deriving a sentiment axis via PCA on point-word vectors. Unlike Turney (2002), it computes sentiment orientations for all vocabulary words rather than only phrase patterns and is claimed to outperform PMI-IR while being robust to data sparsity and smaller datasets. These points clearly differentiate the contribution from prior work and represent a non-trivial methodological shift, though the approach builds on existing vector-space ideas rather than introducing an entirely new theoretical framework.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a notable novel use of distributional semantic models—building a high-dimensional word embedding space, using PCA to derive a sentiment axis, and defining sentiment orientation as the difference in cosine distance to averaged positive/negative reference vectors—which is different from Turney’s PMI-IR phrase-based approach. It clearly differentiates its contribution (unsupervised Word2Vec-based method applicable to all vocabulary words and robust to smaller datasets) from prior work, and while it builds on existing embedding techniques, the specific pipeline (PCA-derived axis + reference vectors + SO formulation) is a non-trivial and informative extension that would be surprising to experts familiar only with PMI-IR.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem—measuring word-level sentiment orientation—and proposes an unsupervised Word2Vec-based method that reportedly outperforms the classic PMI-IR approach while being robust to data sparsity and usable with much smaller datasets (IMDB, SST). By computing sentiment orientations for all vocabulary items (not only phrase patterns) and demonstrating both unsupervised and semi‑supervised evaluations, the work is likely to change how researchers approach unsupervised sentiment induction and to be built upon in subsequent work. Its practical advantages (smaller data requirements, robustness) suggest strong impact beyond a niche contribution, though it stops short of claiming transformational, field‑wide upheaval. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of measuring sentiment orientation with an unsupervised embedding-based method (Word2Vec) and is evaluated on standard datasets (IMDB, Stanford Sentiment Treebank). It reportedly outperforms the classic unsupervised PMI-IR approach while using much smaller data and being robust to data sparsity, and it generalizes sentiment orientation to all vocabulary items rather than only extracted phrases. These facts suggest a strong, broadly useful contribution that researchers and practitioners in sentiment analysis are likely to adopt and extend, making a substantial (but not obviously transformative) impact on the area.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—measuring word-level sentiment orientation—and proposes an unsupervised Word2Vec-based method that reportedly outperforms the classic PMI-IR approach while being robust to data sparsity and usable with much smaller datasets (IMDB, SST). By computing sentiment orientations for all vocabulary items (not only phrase patterns) and demonstrating both unsupervised and semi‑supervised evaluations, the work is likely to change how researchers approach unsupervised sentiment induction and to be built upon in subsequent work. Its practical advantages (smaller data requirements, robustness) suggest strong impact beyond a niche contribution, though it stops short of claiming transformational, field‑wide upheaval.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of measuring sentiment orientation with an unsupervised embedding-based method (Word2Vec) and is evaluated on standard datasets (IMDB, Stanford Sentiment Treebank). It reportedly outperforms the classic unsupervised PMI-IR approach while using much smaller data and being robust to data sparsity, and it generalizes sentiment orientation to all vocabulary items rather than only extracted phrases. These facts suggest a strong, broadly useful contribution that researchers and practitioners in sentiment analysis are likely to adopt and extend, making a substantial (but not obviously transformative) impact on the area.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper’s experimental comparison is limited to the classic PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) baseline and reports outperforming it while claiming robustness to sparsity and smaller data. However, the PMI-IR replication was only applicable to a reduced subset of reviews (predefined patterns) and evaluation metrics are not specified, so the comparisons are partially unfair and incomplete. Although the methodology differences are explained and related work coverage is described as thorough, the paper omits experimental comparisons to more recent or supervised sentiment approaches, so the empirical evaluation is only moderately adequate. | B(3): The paper evaluates against the classic PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) baseline and reports outperforming it, but that is an old/limited comparator and no more recent embedding-based or other competitive baselines are tested. Comparisons appear imperfectly fair: PMI-IR could only be applied to a reduced subset of IMDB reviews (9,613 -> 7,646), and evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary. The authors do acknowledge PMI-IR’s data-sparsity limitations and clearly analyze methodological differences (phrase-oriented PMI-IR vs their word-level, Word2Vec-based approach), and the related work is described as thorough, but empirical coverage of the most relevant modern baselines is missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s experimental comparison is limited to the classic PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) baseline and reports outperforming it while claiming robustness to sparsity and smaller data. However, the PMI-IR replication was only applicable to a reduced subset of reviews (predefined patterns) and evaluation metrics are not specified, so the comparisons are partially unfair and incomplete. Although the methodology differences are explained and related work coverage is described as thorough, the paper omits experimental comparisons to more recent or supervised sentiment approaches, so the empirical evaluation is only moderately adequate.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against the classic PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) baseline and reports outperforming it, but that is an old/limited comparator and no more recent embedding-based or other competitive baselines are tested. Comparisons appear imperfectly fair: PMI-IR could only be applied to a reduced subset of IMDB reviews (9,613 -> 7,646), and evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary. The authors do acknowledge PMI-IR’s data-sparsity limitations and clearly analyze methodological differences (phrase-oriented PMI-IR vs their word-level, Word2Vec-based approach), and the related work is described as thorough, but empirical coverage of the most relevant modern baselines is missing.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and partially supported by experiments comparing the proposed Word2Vec/PCA-based SO measure to PMI-IR on IMDB and SST datasets, and the core formulation (SO(w)=CosDst(Vec_pos,w)-CosDst(Vec_neg,w)) is mathematically coherent. However, important experimental details are missing (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters not reported, no mention of statistical tests) and the PMI-IR replication reduced the test set due to pattern requirements, which could bias comparisons. The use of PCA to identify a sentiment axis and averaging vectors for positive/negative references is plausible but rests on unstated assumptions (linear separability, choice of PCA dimensions) that are not fully justified. Overall, the results are promising but the experimental methodology and some assumptions leave gaps that prevent full technical validation of the claims. | B(3): The main claims are clearly stated and the core method (computing SO(w)=CosDst(Vec_pos,w)-CosDst(Vec_neg,w) after PCA) is mathematically plausible, but empirical support is incomplete because evaluation metrics are not specified and hyperparameters/code are not reported. The experimental methodology is weakly documented: only PMI-IR is used as a baseline, no statistical tests are mentioned, and the PMI-IR replication reduced the test set (9,613 to 7,646), which could bias comparisons. Consequently, the conclusion that the approach outperforms PMI-IR and is robust to data sparsity is not fully justified by the evidence provided in the summary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and partially supported by experiments comparing the proposed Word2Vec/PCA-based SO measure to PMI-IR on IMDB and SST datasets, and the core formulation (SO(w)=CosDst(Vec_pos,w)-CosDst(Vec_neg,w)) is mathematically coherent. However, important experimental details are missing (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters not reported, no mention of statistical tests) and the PMI-IR replication reduced the test set due to pattern requirements, which could bias comparisons. The use of PCA to identify a sentiment axis and averaging vectors for positive/negative references is plausible but rests on unstated assumptions (linear separability, choice of PCA dimensions) that are not fully justified. Overall, the results are promising but the experimental methodology and some assumptions leave gaps that prevent full technical validation of the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and the core method (computing SO(w)=CosDst(Vec_pos,w)-CosDst(Vec_neg,w) after PCA) is mathematically plausible, but empirical support is incomplete because evaluation metrics are not specified and hyperparameters/code are not reported. The experimental methodology is weakly documented: only PMI-IR is used as a baseline, no statistical tests are mentioned, and the PMI-IR replication reduced the test set (9,613 to 7,646), which could bias comparisons. Consequently, the conclusion that the approach outperforms PMI-IR and is robust to data sparsity is not fully justified by the evidence provided in the summary.

main_claims: Introduces a novel approach using distributional semantic (vector space/word-embedding) models to measure the sentiment orientation of words.
Presents an unsupervised (Word2Vec-based) methodology that outperforms the previous unsupervised PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) approach while using much smaller datasets and being robust to data sparsity.
Unlike Turney's phrase-oriented approach, their method computes sentiment orientations for all words in the vocabulary.
They evaluate both unsupervised and semi-supervised conditions and compare results against the existing unsupervised PMI-IR method.
_summary.methodology: Build a high-dimensional vector space from the IMDB dataset using point words (adjectives/adverbs), compute pairwise cosine distances, apply PCA to reduce to two dimensions to find a sentiment axis, construct positive/negative reference vectors by averaging, and define a word's sentiment orientation as the difference between cosine distances to the two reference vectors (SO(w)=CosDst(Vec_pos,w)-CosDst(Vec_neg,w)); review polarity is the sign of the mean SO over its words.
limitations: PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) requires a sufficiently large corpus to compute reliable PMI and suffers from data sparsity.
In their PMI-IR replication, the method is only applicable to reviews containing predefined patterns, reducing the test set from 9,613 to 7,646 posts.
